title: Theory of quantum decoherence in macroscopic topological insulators
authors: Xian-Peng Zhang, Yan-Qing Feng, Wanxiang Feng, Yugui Yao
abstract: Quantum decoherence-the loss of quantum coherence due to interactions with an environment-plays a central role in quantum transport, and controlling this ubiquitous yet inevitable phenomenon is essential for practical quantum technologies. Despite its importance, the microscopic mechanisms of decoherence in infinite-size topological insulators remain poorly understood. Here, we develop a comprehensive theory that quantitatively investigates how quantum decoherence shapes the quantum spin Hall effect in macroscopic topological insulators, and reveal that decoherence-induced corrections scale quadratically with impurity density. Besides, we uncover a previously unidentified mechanism of the extrinsic spin Hall effect: a second-order skew-scattering process intrinsically tied to quantum decoherence-fundamentally distinct from, yet substantially stronger than, the conventional third-order skew-scattering mechanism. Furthermore, we predict a new scaling law in which the decoherence-induced spin Hall conductivity scales quadratically with the longitudinal conductivity, providing a clear experimental signature of decoherence effects. Our results establish the essential role of decoherence in quantum transport of topological insulators and reveal that macroscopic topological insulators offer a promising platform for next-generation spintronic applications.
summary: This paper develops a theory to explain how quantum decoherence affects the spin Hall effect in large-scale topological insulators. It reveals a new, stronger mechanism for the extrinsic spin Hall effect and predicts a specific scaling law that can be used to experimentally detect decoherence effects. This work highlights the importance of decoherence in the development of future spintronic technologies.
main_problem: Understanding the microscopic mechanisms of quantum decoherence and its impact on the quantum spin Hall effect in macroscopic, infinite-size topological insulators.
main_result: The authors identify a new second-order skew-scattering mechanism for the extrinsic spin Hall effect tied to decoherence and predict a new scaling law where spin Hall conductivity scales quadratically with longitudinal conductivity.
method: Development of a comprehensive theoretical framework to quantitatively investigate decoherence-induced corrections in topological transport.
model_or_system: Macroscopic topological insulators subject to interactions with an environment and impurity-induced scattering.
key_observables: Quantum spin Hall conductivity, longitudinal conductivity, and impurity density scaling.
important_parameters: Impurity density and the relationship between spin Hall and longitudinal conductivity.
main_assumptions: The theory focuses on the regime of macroscopic, infinite-size topological insulators.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of decoherence in topological insulators, develops a theory for decoherence-induced corrections, identifies a new skew-scattering mechanism, and concludes with a predicted experimental scaling law.
why_interesting: It provides a new perspective on how environmental coupling (decoherence) fundamentally alters transport properties and introduces a new mechanism for the spin Hall effect through second-order scattering.
